title: Strengths and limitations of 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts | Stats NZ
content:
## Strengths and limitations of 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts
22 June 2021, 10:45am
E ngā mana, e ngā reo, tēnei te reo mihi ki ngā pari kārangaranga o te motu, tēnā koutou katoa.

## Purpose and overview
This document provides guidance on how to use the Iwi affiliation (estimated counts): 2018 data. This data has been released through the joint work of Stats NZ and the Data Iwi Leaders Group (Data ILG) of the National Iwi Chairs Forum.
For many purposes, the 2018 iwi estimates provide a more relevant and up-to-date picture for iwi than the continued use of 2013 Census iwi data. The 2018 iwi estimates are not official 2018 Census counts and the quality of these estimates ranges from moderate to poor depending on how the data is used. This document provides guidance on the care required when using this data.
The 2018 iwi estimates are useful for providing:
- updated population counts for all iwi
- a more recent representation of iwi characteristics (for example, age and income) compared with the 2013 Census
- information for the 32 iwi who were added to the Stats NZ iwi statistical classification as part of the 2017 review .
The 2018 iwi estimates are not recommended for:
- comparing counts with previous census years
- measuring small populations (of less than 100 people).
Background Where to find the data About the data How to use your iwi data Additional characteristics Further help
Stats NZ was unable to publish official statistical counts of iwi from the 2018 Census as the iwi data did not meet an acceptable quality standard.
Since then, Stats NZ and the Data ILG have worked together to provide iwi with usable data about their populations from the 2018 Census. Data ILG has played a critical role in the evaluation and analysis of the quality of 2018 Census iwi data. The significance of Data ILG’s contribution is such that Stats NZ would not have arrived at this position without their involvement.
Alongside this work on behalf of Data ILG, Te Kāhui Raraunga Charitable Trust (TKR) designed and developed an iwi data platform, Te Whata, to host and visualise datasets to enable iwi to have a single view of how iwi are doing. This document is intended to support users of the data displayed on Te Whata and available on Stats NZ.
Throughout this document ‘we’ refers to Stats NZ and Data ILG in acknowledgement of the joint work carried out to produce this data.

## Where to find the data
The 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts data is available on:
- Te Whata iwi data platform – key indicators and charts
- Iwi affiliation (estimated counts): 2018
- NZ.Stat tables – public release of data tables
- Te Ara Takatū – customised iwi datasets available from Stats NZ
- Integrated data infrastructure – for researchers.
The data provides estimates of the iwi population count and estimates of iwi population characteristics, such as age, sex, or income, for each iwi in the Stats NZ iwi classification. For example, we have produced estimates for the number of males and females for each iwi. Throughout this document ‘iwi estimates’ refers to the estimates provided from this data.
The iwi estimates are of people who identified as being of Māori descent in the 2018 Census. They are an estimate based on available information sources and statistical adjustments of the data using accepted methodologies. Stats NZ often produce estimates, for example survey outputs like Te Kupenga , but this is the first time that Stats NZ has released these types of estimates from a population census.
To produce the 2018 iwi estimates, we used the 2018 Census iwi responses and, where possible, where iwi responses were missing we used either their 2013 Census response or the iwi affiliation/s of their parents (for children under 15 years). We then used statistical methods to adjust for the remaining missing data.
The methods applied to the 2018 Census data were designed specifically for the high levels of missing data. It is not expected that these methods will be applied in future censuses. For the 2023 Census, Stats NZ are developing a number of activities to help drive higher response rates for Māori and iwi.
Methodology for the 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts contains further details on the methods we used to produce these estimates.

## How to use your iwi data
This section provides general guidance on when this data is useful and when further consideration of the quality is required.
As with all estimates, there is uncertainty due to the adjustments applied. For example, if the estimate is 100 people then the actual number of people may be somewhere between 80 and 120. The uncertainty of the estimates increases as the size of the estimate becomes smaller. That is, the reliability is lower for small counts (less than 100 people). It is important you consider the level of uncertainty when using this data.
We recommend you also read the following documents, before using this data to make key decisions for your iwi:
- 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts – DataInfo+ provides a detailed description of the quality of this data and measures of uncertainty.
- Iwi affiliation (estimated counts): 2018 data sources and quality by iwi provides key quality information for each iwi.

### Population size of your iwi
For most iwi, the 2018 iwi estimates provide a more accurate estimate of the population size of an iwi than the 2013 Census counts. This is due to improvements in measuring the number of people of Māori descent, the 2018 Census being more recent than 2013, and the use of the updated (2017) iwi statistical classifications.
For some iwi, issues have been identified that have impacted the quality of their estimated population size. These issues have been noted in Te Whata and in Iwi affiliation (estimated counts): 2018 data sources and quality by iwi .

### Population characteristics of your iwi
For characteristics about your iwi, for example age, income, or where your iwi live, the 2018 data is useful as it provides a more up-to-date picture than the 2013 Census data. The characteristics published as part of this release were chosen for their higher levels of quality compared to other census characteristics. You should be aware that the characteristics for your iwi are based on census responses, alternative data sources, and the estimation process.
The accuracy of your iwi estimates reduces for smaller population groups. This will impact the usefulness of the data for many iwi looking to understand aspects such as where their people are located within Aotearoa New Zealand. While further geographic estimates for your iwi can be provided through Te Ara Takatū , detailed geographic information is unlikely to be fit for purpose (except for very large iwi).
This will also impact characteristics of smaller population groups, such as the number of people in your iwi over 80 years of age.
2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts – DataInfo+ provides guidelines on how accurate the estimates are for different sized groups in specific areas. It also provides information on the data sources used to produce the estimates.

### Comparing iwi characteristics with the 2018 Māori descent population
You can use the 2018 iwi estimates to compare characteristics of your iwi to the characteristics of the 2018 Census Māori descent population results. It should be noted, however, that for some characteristics (for example, highest qualification) the Māori descent data may have higher levels of missing data than the iwi estimates.
2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts – DataInfo+ provides further advice on the quality of iwi characteristics.

### Comparing iwi population sizes and characteristics
You can use the 2018 iwi estimates to compare your iwi’s estimated population size and characteristics with other iwi estimates. However, as the quality varies by iwi, we recommend you read the quality information provided for each iwi.

### Comparing 2018 iwi estimates with 2013 Census data
We do not recommend using these iwi estimates to measure population growth in your iwi since 2013 due to:
- the large increase in Māori descent count since 2013 due to improvements in how the 2018 Census measured Māori descent
- changes to the iwi classification since 2013
- changes to the 2018 Census online form
- changes in census methodology between 2013 and 2018 (for example, using government administrative data).
The increase in the overall Māori descent count since 2013 flows through to iwi counts. For most iwi, estimates of their population size are between 20 and 40 percent higher than their 2013 Census counts.
The reasons above also mean caution is required when comparing characteristics of your iwi with data from the 2013 Census. Using percentages rather than counts may help with this, however, care is still required. Any observed changes may be due to the changes outlined above, rather than real change for your iwi.

### Allocating resources or informing Waitangi Tribunal decisions
The 2018 iwi estimates should be used with caution when informing important decisions. We do not recommend the 2018 iwi estimates are used to inform decisions for population groups of less than 100. Please read the specific quality information provided for your iwi and assess the level of uncertainty in the data.
For iwi recently added to the classification, we do not have 2013 Census counts to compare against. This means we are less likely to have been able to identify any issues affecting the quality of data for these iwi. The estimates for these iwi should therefore be treated with caution and used alongside other information for informing important decisions.

### Comparing 2018 iwi estimated counts with your iwi register
There may be differences between the iwi estimates and iwi registers for several reasons:
- Iwi affiliation census data is based solely on self-identification, whereas most iwi registration databases are based on whakapapa that is usually verified and endorsed by an acknowledged authority.
- The level of maintenance of each register (for example, adding new births and removing those who have died) will also contribute towards differences.
- Iwi registers include all iwi members no matter where they live in the world, whereas the 2018 iwi estimates only include those living in Aotearoa New Zealand at the time of the 2018 Census.
- Individuals can register with as many iwi as they can whakapapa to, whereas in the census an individual can only affiliate with up to five iwi.

## Additional characteristics
No further 2018 iwi estimates are planned to be released on the Stats NZ website, but further data is available through Te Ara Takatū . Support and further quality advice will be provided to assist you in using any additional data.
Stats NZ have also loaded the 2018 Census iwi affiliation data into the Integrated data infrastructure (IDI) . Given the joint nature of this work and quality of the data, Data ILG will provide guidance to Stats NZ when assessing IDI applications requesting this dataset.
Data ILG and Stats NZ will look to produce a range of time series using this data within the IDI to support iwi. An example of this is the Job Seeker Support series on Te Whata .
For more help using this data please contact TeTohu@stats.govt.nz .
